text.title: Micro-MVP Process
text.summary: Six-step process for designing, running, and acting on a micro-MVP experiment.

rule.statement: Step 1: Identify the riskiest assumption — the one belief, if wrong, that kills the product idea entirely.
rule.rationale: Testing a secondary assumption first wastes time. Riskiest assumption first forces prioritisation by existential risk, not build convenience.

rule.statement: Step 2: Design the smallest experiment that can falsify or confirm that single assumption. One assumption per experiment.
rule.rationale: Multi-assumption experiments produce ambiguous results. If the experiment fails, you do not know which assumption was wrong.

rule.statement: Step 3: Define success metrics before starting, not after seeing results. Specify the threshold that means "confirmed" vs "rejected".
rule.rationale: Defining metrics after seeing results produces motivated reasoning. Pre-commitment makes the experiment falsifiable.

rule.statement: Step 4: Run the experiment in hours to days. If it takes longer than one week, it is not a micro-MVP — split or simplify.
rule.rationale: Speed is the whole point. A week-long micro-MVP competes with a two-week sprint and loses the argument for the lighter method.

rule.statement: Step 5: Analyse results against the pre-defined threshold. Binary outcome: confirmed or rejected.
rule.rationale: Avoiding a clear verdict extends limbo. Even a failed experiment is valuable — it redirects effort before a full build.

rule.statement: Step 6: Decide: pivot (change the assumption and test again), persevere (move to building), or test the next riskiest assumption.
rule.rationale: One confirmed micro-MVP is not enough to build. Stack experiments until the top 2–3 existential assumptions are validated before committing to a sprint.

antipattern.description: Running a micro-MVP for an assumption already validated by strong existing evidence.
antipattern.reason: Over-testing wastes time. Reserve micro-MVPs for genuinely uncertain assumptions; skip experiments where evidence already converges.

antipattern.description: Skipping the pre-defined success threshold and interpreting results after the fact.
antipattern.reason: Post-hoc interpretation always finds a way to confirm the hypothesis. Pre-commitment is what makes the experiment honest.
